top_gaps: Does not propose practical mitigation strategies or quantify how much real data is needed to arrest collapse.
Limited to a single medical domain and does not test mitigation strategies or generalize findings across multiple clinical specialties.
Curation oracle assumed; does not address how to curate without access to ground-truth quality signals in real deployment.
Correction signal requires an external quality oracle that may itself be AI-generated in practice.
Analysis focuses on language models and Gaussian models; collapse dynamics for multimodal and instruction-tuned models remain underexplored.
Assumes a reliable preference oracle; does not address what happens when preference labels are themselves AI-generated.
Evaluated only on image generation; applicability to language model collapse and diverse modalities is unverified.
Surplexity threshold selection is heuristic and its behavior under adversarial or distribution-shifted synthetic data is untested.
Focuses on image data and continual learning; does not address text-domain synthetic contamination or static training corpus effects.
Theory is primarily analytical and lacks large-scale empirical validation across diverse model architectures and training regimes.
Does not measure downstream impact on model training quality or track how contamination proportion changes over time beyond 2024.
Does not assess content quality, downstream training impact, or whether AI-assisted text differs meaningfully from purely human text for model training purposes.
Does not track temporal trends longitudinally or examine how filtering criteria evolve as AI content grows.
Does not quantify the rate at which retrieval collapse progresses or propose robust mitigation strategies.
Does not propose scalable automated solutions for ongoing monitoring of AI content in online education platforms.
Does not evaluate mitigation strategies for maintaining retrieval quality in AI-polluted web environments.
Does not quantify the scale of AI wildlife content online or propose detection or governance mechanisms.
Lacks longitudinal tracking and does not address how editorial practices might adapt to increasing LLM integration.
Relies on qualitative and anecdotal evidence without rigorous quantitative measurement of AI vs. human content ratios.
Does not examine long-term effects of AI political imagery on voter behavior or propose platform-level countermeasures.
Focuses on binary detection rather than measuring temporal growth trends or effects on downstream media quality.
Does not evaluate robustness to adversarial paraphrasing or cross-domain generalization of detectors.
Does not address watermark robustness against paraphrasing or adversarial removal.
Retrieval defense may itself be polluted as AI content saturates the web, undermining its long-term viability.
Perplexity-based detection may degrade as models improve and AI-generated code becomes lower-perplexity relative to human code.
Surveys existing methods but does not evaluate detector robustness under adversarial conditions or at web scale.
Does not address the feedback loop where undetected AI text enters training data and degrades future detectors.
Does not address detector brittleness or adversarial robustness against evolving generative models.
Does not evaluate watermark persistence under paraphrasing attacks or downstream recursive training on watermarked outputs.
Does not address long-term effectiveness as LLMs improve or the scalability of the watermarking scheme at web scale.
Does not quantify how much diversity loss translates into downstream task performance degradation or model collapse in the strict distributional sense.
Does not address how authenticity metrics behave under recursive or iterative generative training regimes relevant to model collapse.
Focuses on a single language corpus and does not connect diversity metrics to AI-generated content or model training implications.
Does not examine how these diversity metrics change when corpora are contaminated with or replaced by AI-generated text.
Does not examine how these diversity metrics respond to synthetic or AI-generated text infiltration in training corpora.
Does not address data quality, corpus diversity, or the implications of synthetic data for training such models.
Does not consider the impact of AI-generated text on complexity index stability or corpus quality in NLP training scenarios.
Does not address AI-generated text or synthetic data contexts, limiting applicability to model-training quality metrics.
Does not examine recursive training on model-generated translations or downstream effects on model collapse.
Framework is largely theoretical and does not provide practical metrics or empirical benchmarks for real-world generative model evaluation pipelines.
Does not systematically ablate the contribution of CoT data versus base web data quality on downstream reasoning gains.
Findings may not generalize beyond Romanian or other morphologically rich low-resource languages.
Data curation details are described at a high level without ablations isolating the impact of individual filtering steps.
Does not propose a robust alternative filtering method that avoids the identified illusion.
Long-term effects of training exclusively on synthetic data for low-resource languages remain unstudied.
Does not provide a validated alternative experimental protocol for reliable small-to-large transfer of data recipe conclusions.
Does not evaluate extraction quality improvements across non-English or low-resource web corpora.
The trade-off analysis is conducted at pretraining scale but not validated under continual learning or fine-tuning regimes.
Does not address how sample-wise mixing scales with extremely large corpora or interacts with deduplication pipelines.
Does not fully resolve optimal domain mixing ratios or address temporal drift in web data quality.
The constitutional principles themselves still require human authorship, and the approach may not generalize to subtle social harms requiring authentic behavioral data.
Does not investigate whether authentic human behavioral data could provide the social warmth signal without the reliability degradation.
Principles still require human authorship and may embed biases; lacks evaluation on nuanced social or empathetic tasks.
Annotations focus on harm/helpfulness dichotomy and do not capture nuanced social or empathetic signals needed for richer alignment.
Does not address the quality and provenance of the human preference signals used for ranking.
Reward model quality still depends on the quality of principle specification, leaving open how to elicit authentic human values.
Does not address scalability of human feedback collection or the quality degradation of human annotations under large-scale or crowdsourced settings.
Assumes high-quality human preference labels are available; does not address how data quality or authenticity of preferences affects alignment outcomes.
Does not address how AI-generated feedback quality degrades over iterated training cycles or contributes to model collapse dynamics.
Does not quantify the cost-quality tradeoffs of human vs. AI feedback at scale or address temporal degradation of preference data.
Predates LLM alignment concerns and does not address consent, provenance verification, or use of behavioral data for AI training.
Does not address data provenance, consent transparency, or how collected data could be used for AI training governance.
Does not provide a technical implementation of the proposed standards or measure downstream effects on model quality.
Does not evaluate impact on model quality or address scalability to web-scale datasets.
Domain-specific focus on genomics limits generalizability to broader AI training data governance contexts.
Does not address how citizen science data governance frameworks could be adapted for AI training data collection pipelines.
Does not propose concrete technical or governance mechanisms to implement contributor-centered data control at scale.
Focused on biomedical context and does not address implications for AI training data provenance or consent pipelines.
Narrowly focused on mental health measurement tools and does not generalize to broader AI training data provenance challenges.
Does not address scalable technical implementation or applicability to AI training data governance beyond the archaeological/Indigenous data context.
Does not assess temporal drift in web quality or the effect of AI-generated content entering the web corpus over time.
Does not examine how increasing AI-generated web content may degrade future crawl quality over time.
Covers only a single snapshot, leaving temporal evolution of web corpus quality unexamined.
Relies on imperfect detectors and does not quantify downstream impact on LLMs trained on Wikipedia-derived corpora.
Focused on privacy policies specifically, not general web content quality or AI-generated content trends.
Does not measure how consent restrictions affect downstream model quality or training dynamics.
Does not connect web consolidation trends to implications for AI training data diversity or quality.
Focused on event extraction methodology rather than measuring web quality drift or AI content contamination.
Does not address how AI-generated or automated content may affect future privacy policy language or quality.
Does not measure content quality or AI-generated content proportion in the observed web changes.
Does not isolate the specific data characteristics (authentic vs. synthetic) responsible for ToM improvements.
Does not provide empirical benchmarks to operationalize its checklist recommendations.
Does not address whether continuously collected real-world behavioral signals could sustain these gains at deployment scale.
Does not address whether models genuinely reason about social dynamics or rely on surface-level patterns.
Does not explore whether scaling or inference-time compute could close the social reasoning gap.
Does not evaluate whether the alignment generalizes to open-ended real-world social scenarios beyond controlled benchmarks.
Does not address methods for improving ToM performance, only evaluation.
Does not systematically quantify the data requirements or scaling behavior needed for robust social reasoning.
Benchmark construction relies on frontier proprietary MLLMs, raising questions about AI-generated data quality; does not study inference-time scaling as a remedy for cross-cultural gaps.
Does not examine whether improvements generalize beyond the experimental paradigms present in the training data.
Does not rigorously ablate the quality vs. quantity trade-off of self-generated instructions.
Does not systematically study how data quality vs. quantity affects alignment outcomes.
Does not examine whether the quality-over-quantity finding holds at larger scales or for diverse task distributions.
Does not address potential quality degradation or bias introduced by LLM-generated complex instructions.
Does not explore how the selection criteria interact with domain shift or distribution mismatch across tasks.
Does not test whether hybrid gains transfer to genuine theory-of-mind or behavioral prediction tasks beyond response quality metrics.
Does not evaluate whether synthetic empathy data transfers to genuine theory-of-mind or behavioral prediction benchmarks beyond surface response quality.
Does not address data composition ablations for fine-tuning or how in-context alignment scales with data quality versus quantity.
Focuses on dialogue quality perception rather than downstream task performance or systematic quality-versus-quantity scaling laws.
Does not isolate the role of authentic human behavioral data versus synthetic data within each training stage.
Does not address whether test-time scaling generalizes beyond mathematical/logical reasoning to social or empathetic reasoning tasks.
Does not evaluate whether RL-induced reasoning gains extend to social commonsense or theory-of-mind benchmarks.
Does not examine whether test-time compute advantages hold for social reasoning or human-preference alignment tasks.
Does not address whether CoT gains are robust across social reasoning or theory-of-mind tasks, nor how it interacts with data quality or composition.
Focuses on structured logical decomposition of ToM; does not evaluate cross-cultural or ecologically valid social scenarios, nor compare against data-centric improvements.
Does not investigate social reasoning or theory-of-mind tasks specifically, and leaves open how self-consistency interacts with training data quality or composition.
Addresses formal logical reasoning rather than naturalistic social or theory-of-mind reasoning, leaving open whether symbolic scaffolding helps in open-ended social cognition tasks.
